questions.q: Define mean position of a particle.
questions.answer: The mean position of a particle is given by the average value of the position of the particle over a given time interval.
key_points: Mean position is the average value of position

questions.q: State the formula for mean value theorem.
questions.answer: The formula for mean value theorem is ∫[a,b] f(x)dx = f(c)(b-a), where c is the mean value.

questions.q: What is meant by the average value of a function?
questions.answer: The average value of a function f(x) over the interval [a, b] is the value of the function at some point c in the interval, such that ∫[a,b] f(x)dx = f(c)(b-a).

questions.q: Define the work done by a variable force.
questions.answer: The work done by a variable force is given by the integral of the force with respect to displacement.
key_points: Work done by a variable force

questions.q: State the relation between work done and potential energy.
questions.answer: The work done on an object is equal to the change in its potential energy.
key_points: Relation between work done and potential energy

questions.q: What is meant by the centre of mass of a system of particles?
questions.answer: The centre of mass of a system of particles is the point where the entire mass of the system can be considered to be concentrated.
key_points: Centre of mass of a system of particles

questions.q: State the importance of centre of mass in physics.
questions.answer: The centre of mass is important in physics as it helps in understanding the motion of a system of particles.
key_points: Importance of centre of mass in physics

questions.q: Define the moment of a force about a point.
questions.answer: The moment of a force about a point is the product of the force and the perpendicular distance from the point to the line of action of the force.
key_points: Moment of a force about a point

questions.q: State the relation between torque and moment.
questions.answer: Torque is equal to the moment of a force about a point.

questions.q: What are the applications of application of integrals in physics?
questions.answer: The applications of application of integrals in physics include calculating work done, potential energy, centre of mass, moment of a force, and torque.
key_points: Applications of application of integrals in physics

questions.q: Derive the formula for work done by a variable force.
questions.answer: The work done by a variable force is given by the integral of the force with respect to displacement, i.e. W = ∫[a,b] F(x)dx.
key_points: Formula for work done by a variable force

questions.q: Explain the concept of centre of mass with an example.
questions.answer: The centre of mass of a system of particles is the point where the entire mass of the system can be considered to be concentrated. For example, consider a dumbbell with two particles of equal mass attached to a string. The centre of mass lies on the string, equidistant from the two particles.
key_points: Concept of centre of mass with an example

questions.q: Derive the formula for moment of a force about a point.
questions.answer: The moment of a force about a point is the product of the force and the perpendicular distance from the point to the line of action of the force, i.e. M = F × d.
key_points: Formula for moment of a force about a point

questions.q: Explain the relation between work done and potential energy with an example.
questions.answer: The work done on an object is equal to the change in its potential energy. For example, consider a ball being lifted from the ground to a certain height. The work done on the ball is equal to the increase in its potential energy.
key_points: Relation between work done and potential energy with an example

questions.q: Derive the formula for torque and explain its importance in physics.
questions.answer: Torque is equal to the moment of a force about a point, i.e. τ = F × d. Torque is important in physics as it helps in understanding rotational motion.
key_points: Formula for torque and importance in physics

questions.q: Explain the concept of centre of mass with a numerical example.
questions.answer: Consider a system of two particles of masses 2 kg and 3 kg, located at (1, 2) and (4, 5) respectively. The centre of mass is given by (x, y) = ((2 × 1 + 3 × 4)/(2 + 3), (2 × 2 + 3 × 5)/(2 + 3)) = (17/5, 17/5).
key_points: Concept of centre of mass with a numerical example

questions.q: Derive the formula for work done by a variable force and explain its importance in physics.
questions.answer: The work done by a variable force is given by the integral of the force with respect to displacement, i.e. W = ∫[a,b] F(x)dx. Work done is important in physics as it helps in understanding the motion of objects.
key_points: Formula for work done by a variable force and importance in physics
